metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Supplement of BGP-LS Distribution for SR Policies and State
Export of MPLS Network Action (MNA) Information in IPFIX
Signaling Optimization Objective and Bounded Metrics for MPLS Fast Reroute Backup LSP Tunnels
Carrying NRP related Information in MPLS Packets
Operations, Administration and Maintenance (OAM) for Network Resource Partition (NRP) in MPLS Network
Simple Two-Way Active Measurement Protocol (STAMP) Extensions for Reflecting STAMP Packet MPLS Extension Headers
02 - Carrying NRP related Information in MPLS Packets
03 - STAMP Extensions for Reflecting STAMP Packet MPLS Extension Headers
04 - Supplement of BGP-LS Distribution for SR Policies and State
05 - Export of MPLS Network Action (MNA) Information in IPFIX
06 - Signaling Optimization Objective and Bounded Metrics for MPLS Fast Reroute Backup LSP Tunnels
07-Operations, Administration and Maintenance (OAM) for Network Resource Partition (NRP) in MPLS Network

Oh the next few minutes I will introduce you to a new draft that talks about enhancements to the MPLS RSEP fast sheet out procedures. My name is Pao Npiram my colleague Abhishek is the other quarter listed on this slide RSEP 49T has been around for more than 2 decades now. The fast sheet procedures defined in this RSEP are widely deployed. RSEP introduced an RSEP object called the fast sheet object which is reproduced here to help jog your memory. It is used for requesting local protection at the PLS that are traversed by the primary path. It also is used to specify what type of local protection is needed to be invoked at each PLR. In addition to this the fast sheet object is also used to carry a limited set of backup path computation constraints like the bandwidth, the hop limit and the resource of entities. There is currently no mechanism for the ingress of a protection desiring LSP to influence the optimization object to that gets used for backup path computation. The same goes for any nuanced constraints like the pounded metrics. Implementation is generally on the local policy that exists on the PLRs to get these additional attributes. What this new draft does is try and plug this gap. It introduces a new TLE based object called fast sheet out extension to carry these additional attributes that can be used by the PLRs for backup path computation. Object itself and the procedures are defined in such a way that the backwards compatibility with the existing implementation stays intact. It is also extensible enough to leave room for any new types of optimization objectives or constraints to be added to the mix if or meant they needed. The next couple of slides quickly go or some terminology that is actually used in this draft. When we say optimization objective or an optimization metric in this draft we are simply referring to a quantifying will metric that a path computational algorithm optimizes for the set of examples space for the draft. The second turn that is extensible used is the bounded metric. When we say bounded metric we are referring to a path computation constrained that is used to define a bound that a computer path the computer T path must satisfy. The fast year extension object must be included only as a companion to the fast year object. What that means is that the ingress must include the fast year extension object only if it is already included the fast year object. There is also provisions made for the ingress to be able to figure out whether a PLR has actually catered to the request or not. This is done by introducing a new RRO sub object flag called a for our extension protection which the PLR would set only if it has successfully catered to the request. In terms of the procedure the PLR, a PLR implementation that is not able to recognize this object it could simply ignore it and forward it as is to the next stop. This is made possible by this would be made possible by the class number that would end up picking. A PLR implementation that is able to recognize the object and is able to successfully cater to the request will set the RSE RRO sub object flag that I talked about earlier to indicate to the ingress that it was successfully able to cater to the request and in for some reason if it is not able to cater to the request it would simply fall back on to the local policy that would exist on the PLR just like it would do today. Okay, here's some recap of the background information.
Firstly we know that the NRP or Natural Resource Partition is defined as a set of
data resources located on the connected set of links in the NN network.
The concept has been introduced in the RFC 9543 and can be used as the NN resource constructs
to support one group of the network slice or enhance vaping services in the RFC 9732.
In the data plane there are different options to indicate where which NRP a package belongs
to.
And here we are talking about the mechanism using a dedicated IDIDF error called NRP Select
RID.
And this document describes a mechanism to carry the NRP information including the NRP Select
RID in the MPH package using the M&A post-dat data.
So that the transient nodes can know which NRP a package belongs to and the NRP specific
package processing and for the 40 can be performed on each hub along the past.
And this PSD-based approach is considered to be complementary to the ISD-based NRP Select
Re-encoding as defining the NNRP Select or draft.

And we also have some optional fields to carry a NRP related information as the answer
the data of this action.

Oh the next few minutes I will introduce you to a new draft that talks about enhancements to the MPLS
RSEP fast sheet out procedures. My name is Pao Npiram my colleague Abhishek
is the other quarter listed on this slide RSEP 49T has been around for more than 2 decades now.
The fast sheet procedures defined in this RSEP are widely deployed.
RSEP introduced an RSEP object called the fast sheet object which is reproduced here to help
jog your memory. It is used for requesting local protection at the PLS that are traversed by the
primary path. It also is used to specify what type of local protection is needed to be
invoked at each PLR. In addition to this the fast sheet object is also used to carry a limited
set of backup path computation constraints like the bandwidth, the hop limit and the resource of
entities. There is currently no mechanism for the ingress of a protection desiring LSP to
influence the optimization object to that gets used for backup path computation. The same goes for
any nuanced constraints like the pounded metrics. Implementation is generally on the local policy
that exists on the PLRs to get these additional attributes. What this new draft does is try and
plug this gap. It introduces a new TLE based object called fast sheet out extension to carry these
additional attributes that can be used by the PLRs for backup path computation.
Object itself and the procedures are defined in such a way that the backwards compatibility
with the existing implementation stays intact. It is also extensible enough to
leave room for any new types of optimization objectives or constraints to be added to the mix
if or meant they needed. The next couple of slides quickly go or some terminology that
is actually used in this draft. When we say optimization objective or an optimization metric in this
draft we are simply referring to a quantifying will metric that a path computational algorithm
optimizes for the set of examples space for the draft. The second turn that is extensible
used is the bounded metric. When we say bounded metric we are referring to a path computation
constrained that is used to define a bound that a computer path the computer T path must satisfy.
This bounded metric can be of links scope. It could be of paths scope.
And again there are examples of this space thread and the draft for each of these categories.
The next couple of slides go or the procedures that come into play when we
introduce this functionality. At the ingress of a production desiring LSP there may be
explicit configuration to specify the optimization objective and the bounded metrics for
backup path computation and if such explicit configuration exists the expectation is that
an ingress implementation that is adhering to the draft would be able to signal these additional
attributes in a new object called the fast year extension object that is carried in the path message.
The fast year extension object must be included only as a companion to the fast year object.
What that means is that the ingress must include the fast year extension object only if it is already
included the fast year object. There is also provisions made for the ingress to be able to
figure out whether a PLR has actually catered to the request or not. This is done by
introducing a new RRO sub object flag called a for our extension protection which the PLR
would set only if it has successfully catered to the request.
In terms of the procedure the PLR, a PLR implementation that is not able to
recognize this object it could simply ignore it and forward it as is to the next stop.
This is made possible by this would be made possible by the class number that would end up picking.
A PLR implementation that is able to recognize the object and is able to successfully
cater to the request will set the RSE RRO sub object flag that I talked about earlier to
indicate to the ingress that it was successfully able to cater to the request and in
for some reason if it is not able to cater to the request it would simply fall back on to the
local policy that would exist on the PLR just like it would do today. In such a scenario the flag
the RSE RRO sub object flag will not be set.
The next four slides go over the protocol extensions and grade detail. I will quickly touch upon some
highlights. As you can see here the fast year extension object contains a series of PLVs.
We have defined a couple of PLVs in this draft. The first of that is the optimization
metric PLV. The higher an arcase is that you would include only one optimization metric for given path.
But the draft has provided procedures to handle scenarios where there is a desire to specify
multiple optimization objectives. The second metric PLV that you have introduced in this draft is
the bounded metric PLV. There is a flag in here the L flag which indicates whether this is
a path scope or a fling scope again that after our scenarios where multiple bounded metric
source specified in this object. The last protocol extension of interest here is the RRO IP4 IPv6
object flag that I alluded to earlier. This is set by the PLR if it is successfully able to
cater to the request captured in the fast year extension object. This is my last slide.
